1:
The chapter opens on the darkest reign in Judah's history, and the witnesses first fix the bare facts of the boy-king before turning to the tragedy they forebode. The reformed John Gill notes that Manasseh was but twelve years old when he began to reign, born three years after Hezekiah's recovery from his sickness, in the seventeenth year of his father's reign. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the accession against the glory just departed: how delightful were the meditations on the last reign, he says, when Jerusalem was a quiet habitation because a city of righteousness; but now the gold is become dim and the most fine gold changed, and we have melancholy work upon our hands. Manasseh began young, twelve years old, born when his father was about forty-two; and being young he was, Henry judges, puffed up with his honour and proud of it, valuing himself upon undoing what his father had done, and easily wrought upon by seducers who lay in wait to deceive, enemies of Hezekiah's reformation who flattered him and used his power at their pleasure. Many, Henry warns, have been undone by coming too soon to their honours and estates.

On the length of the reign the witnesses dwell with a kind of wonder. Henry observes that Manasseh reigned longest of any of the kings of Judah, fifty-five years, and that this was the only very bad reign that was also a long one, Joram's being but eight years and Ahaz's sixteen; though he hopes that in the beginning affairs still moved in the course Hezekiah had left them, and that in the latter end, after his repentance, religion recovered some ground. Part of that long span, Henry and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agree, was spent not on the throne but in a Babylonian prison; Wesley notes tersely that the reckoning of the reign comprehends the years of his imprisonment. Adam Clarke draws the whole arc in advance: Manasseh succeeded his father, reigned fifty-five years, filled Jerusalem and the land with abominable idolatry and murder, was carried captive to Babylon, there repented and was restored, put down idolatry, and died at the age of sixty-seven, a summary Clarke grounds in the fuller record of 2 Chron. 33.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the political and philological background. Manasseh was a tributary of Esarhaddon and Assurbanipal in turn, and his very name, like that of his son Amon, suggests the Egyptian influence popular in that age of combinations against Assyria; his early accession may itself help explain his deviation from his father's religious policy, without any need, against Thenius, to suppose the queen-mother governed until he came of age. The mother's name, Hephzibah, Ellicott adds, means "my delight is in her," and reappears as a title of Zion in Isa. 62:4 — a name of favour set, poignantly, at the head of the reign that would do most to forfeit it.
2:
"He did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, after the abominations of the heathen" — and the witnesses read the verdict as the reversal of everything Hezekiah had built. The reformed John Gill glosses the evil in a word: he was guilty of idolatry. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch press the point that the young king did not choose his father's ways but set up again the idolatry of Ahab, and they name the party that captured him. A godless faction stood at the head of the nation — chiefs, priests, and false prophets who would not hearken to the law of the LORD, and who in Hezekiah's day had sought help against Assyria not from Jehovah but from the Egyptians, as Isaiah had charged in Isa. 28:7 and Isa. 30:9. These men, Keil and Delitzsch argue, obtained control of the young and inexperienced king and persuaded him to bring idolatry back, so that the phrasing echoes the older verdicts on Ahaziah and Ahaz at 2 Kings 8:18 and 2 Kings 16:3.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke keeps the indictment simple and total: Manasseh exactly copied the conduct of those nations which God had cast out of the land, the very peoples whose abominations had earned their expulsion. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees on the source of the corruption, suggesting the king acted perhaps under the pernicious influence of his courtiers, and comparing the parallel case of Rehoboam, another king led astray by his counsellors. The "abominations of the heathen," Ellicott notes, recall the warnings of Deut. 29:17 and the apostasy of Solomon in 1 Kings 11:5; and "the heathen whom the LORD cast out" are the peoples of Canaan, the same nations whose dispossession the historian recalled at 2 Kings 17:8.

The voices do not divide, but together they build a single, sober argument. Where his father had cleansed the land, Manasseh deliberately re-imported the worship of the very nations God had driven out to make room for Israel; the sin is not innovation but imitation, a reaching back to the abominations God had already judged once. Both schools trace the ruin, in part, to the king's youth and the men who surrounded him: a boy of twelve, Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott alike suggest, was clay in the hands of a court that had only been waiting for Hezekiah's death to undo his reforms. Yet none of the witnesses lets that youth excuse him; he did what he could not but know, having been well taught, was evil in the sight of the LORD, and the well-instructed son proved worse than the heathen his fathers had displaced.
3:
The catalogue of Manasseh's idolatry begins here, and the witnesses count its layers one by one. The reformed John Gill starts with the demolition undone: the king built up again the high places which Hezekiah his father had destroyed, re-erecting the temples and altars upon them. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as a deliberate gradation of three distinct apostasies. First, the idolatrous high places, the bamoth, which Hezekiah had thrown down in 2 Kings 18:4; second, altars for Baal and an Asherah, in exact imitation of Ahab of Israel in 1 Kings 16:32-33, the Asherah here being the very image named at v.7; and third, worst and newest, the worship of all the host of heaven, a star-worship not found among the Israelites before the Assyrian era and probably of Assyrian or Chaldaean origin, distinct, they carefully note, from the older Syrophoenician cult in which sun and moon were adored as Baal and Astarte.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses fill in the identity of the idols. Adam Clarke names the "grove" as Asherah, the Babylonian Mylitta or Roman Venus, cross-referencing his note at 2 Kings 17:10, and takes "all the host of heaven" for the stars and planets, and particularly the sun and moon. John Wesley reads the same host as sun, moon, and stars, and hears in the bare verb "he built" an act of filial contempt: in rebuilding what his father had razed, Manasseh was trampling on the dust and affronting the memory of his worthy father.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the philology and the social history. The Hebrew idiom, imitated by the Septuagint and Vulgate, is literally "he returned and built," that is, he rebuilt; and the whole apparatus — the high places, the sun-pillars, the asherim and sacred trees — was, in Robertson Smith's phrase which Ellicott quotes, of the very substance of Israel's worship in the popular sanctuaries, plainly inconsistent with the prophetic faith. The star-worship in particular, Ellicott observes, with its concomitant astrology and superstition, had first been introduced under Ahaz, and would recur in the indictments of 2 Kings 17:16 and 2 Kings 23:12. So the witnesses agree in reading the verse as an ascending scale of revolt: the undoing of reform, the revival of Canaanite and Phoenician idols, and the importation of a wholly new Mesopotamian cult of the heavens into the worship of Judah.
4:
The idolatry now crosses a threshold it should never have touched, the house of the LORD itself, and the witnesses register the enormity. The reformed John Gill locates these altars precisely in the holy place, as distinct from the courts named in the next verse, and reserved for the idols of the Gentiles. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch stress the terrible irony the verse itself frames: he built altars in the very house of which the LORD had said, "In Jerusalem will I put my name." Their reading of the whole passage advances gradatim, by steps, and this is one of the worst, the planting of pagan altars in the dwelling God had chosen for his name.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke puts it with blunt force: he placed idolatrous altars even in the temple. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse as the general statement that the following verse particularises, the altars "in the house" being those set up in its two courts, and hears again the echo of the great promise given to Solomon that in Jerusalem God would put his name, a promise recorded at 1 Kings 14:21 and now flung back in God's face.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the full affront. Manasseh set up his images and altars in the house of the LORD, in the two courts of the temple, in the very house of which God had said to Solomon, "Here will I put my name," as v.7 will repeat. Thus, Henry says, he defied God to his face, and impudently affronted him with his rivals set immediately under his eye, as one who was neither afraid of God's wrath nor ashamed of his own folly. He desecrated what had been consecrated to God, and did, in effect, turn God out of his own house and put the rebels in possession of it; so that the faithful worshippers, coming to the appointed place to perform their duty, found to their grief and terror other gods ready to receive their offerings. The witnesses do not divide. Where the earlier verses had Manasseh corrupting the high places and the land, this one shows him carrying the pollution into the sanctuary, the one place on earth stamped with the name of the living God, and the historian, all agree, sets it down as the point at which contempt for God became open and deliberate defiance.
5:
If the last verse brought strange altars into the temple, this one multiplies them and pushes them deeper in — "altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of the LORD." The reformed John Gill marks the object of this worship plainly: altars raised to the sun, the moon, and the stars, the whole starry host that Manasseh had learned to adore. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fastens on the two courts and finds in them the measure of the sacrilege: not only the outer court but even the inner and more sacred court, the very place where the sacrifices were offered to Jehovah, was now crowded with altars to the heavens. The pollution had reached the holiest ground short of the sanctuary itself.

Keil and Delitzsch, reading the verse together with those around it, see the same relentless advance by degrees: the star-worship, newest and most foreign of Manasseh's idolatries, was not kept at the margins of the city or even at the temple gate, but installed within both courts of God's house, so that the worshipper of Jehovah could not approach his God without passing the altars of the host of heaven. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already gathered the force of it: the images and altars set up in the two courts of the temple were an affront offered to God immediately under his own eye, in the house he had claimed for his name.

The witnesses do not contend over so plain a verse; their common labour is simply to feel its weight. What had begun at v.3 on the high places of the countryside has now climbed, court by court, into the innermost enclosure of the temple. The repetition of "all the host of heaven," so soon after v.3, is not idle; it registers how thoroughly the new astral cult had colonised the worship of Judah, from the open hills to the altar of burnt-offering. The historian, the witnesses agree, dwells on the courts precisely because the horror is not merely that Manasseh worshipped the stars, but that he compelled the God of Israel to share his own house and his own courts with them — a rivalry staged, deliberately, on the one spot where the name of the LORD was to dwell for ever, as the next verse's climax will make explicit.
6:
Idolatry now turns to blood and sorcery, and the witnesses take the crimes in order. The reformed John Gill reads "he made his son pass through the fire" as a dedication to Molech after the manner of the old Canaanites and Phoenicians, his son Amon among them, performed, as the parallel shows, in the valley of the son of Hinnom. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch agree that he offered his son in sacrifice to Moloch as Ahaz had done in 2 Kings 16:3, in the valley of Benhinnom named at 2 Kings 23:10, and that he practised soothsaying and witchcraft of every kind, the terms recalling the prohibitions of Deut. 18:10 and Lev. 19:26; the phrase "dealt with familiar spirits" they render as his appointing a necromancer and wise people to office, against the law of Deut. 18:11.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses press into the vocabulary of the occult. Adam Clarke unpacks each dark art in turn: "observed times" is divination by the clouds, watching their course and kinds and directions; "used enchantments" means incantations, spells, and charms; "dealt with familiar spirits" makes him a necromancer, a raiser of spirits whom he pressed into his service, one who had a Python; and "wizards" are the knowing ones, the white witches, a whole catalogue he refers back to his notes on Lev. 19:26-31. John Wesley reads the fire-rite as a passing between two fires by which the king dedicated his son to Molech in open contempt of the seal of circumcision, by which the child had been dedicated to God; and he takes "observed times" of the superstitious keeping of lucky and unlucky days after the heathen fashion.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the Septuagint and Chronicles read "his sons" in the plural, and that "dealt with familiar spirits" means Manasseh formally appointed such a person as a standing court official, comparing the parallels at 2 Kings 17:17 and 2 Chron. 33:6. From Manasseh onward, he adds, quoting Robertson Smith, Moloch-worship and the worship of the Queen of Heaven appear as prominent new features of Judah's idolatry, and the local high places took on their restoration a more markedly heathen character than before. The verse closes with the historian's own recoil, "he wrought much wickedness in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger," and the witnesses agree that here the reign passes beyond mere false worship into child-sacrifice and traffic with the dead, the two sins that most defile a land and most surely, as the coming oracle will declare, draw down its ruin.
7:
The ascent of impiety reaches its summit here, and the witnesses say so with one voice: the setting of a carved idol inside the temple is the worst of all Manasseh's crimes. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose reading of the chapter advances by degrees, call this the very worst — "he even placed the image of Asherah in the temple," that is, in the Holy Place — for by the express word of the LORD to David and to Solomon the temple was to be the dwelling-place of his name for ever, as 2 Samuel 7:13 and 1 Kings 9:3 had declared. Charles Ellicott concurs that the graven image of the Asherah of v.3 was erected within the Temple itself, probably in the holy place, and calls it flatly the climax of Manasseh's impiety, comparing the later cleansing of 2 Kings 23:4 and the defilement lamented in Ezekiel 43:7 and Jeremiah 7:30.

The reformed John Gill lingers over the identity of the image. Some, he says, take it for an idol standing in a planted grove, or a carved representation of a grove wrought in gold or silver; but the best account, which he draws from Selden, is that it was an image of Asherah, that is, of Astarte or Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Zidonians, whom the Phoenicians called Astroarche and identified with the moon. Gill records the curious traditions that the image had four faces, which some thought a mimicry of the cherubim, while Suidas reports it a statue of Jupiter, likewise four-faced. Whatever its form, its place was the offence.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke insists on the one point that governs the whole: "every one may see that Asherah here must signify an idol, and not a grove," a case he promises to prove at length in his observations at the chapter's end. John Wesley alone inclines a little differently, taking the image to be the image of that Baal which was worshipped in the grove rather than of Asherah herself, a small divergence within a larger agreement. For the witnesses do not really divide over what matters: whether the idol be named Astarte or Baal, moon-goddess or four-faced Jupiter, the scandal is that a graven thing was lodged in the house which God had chosen out of all the tribes of Israel to put his name there for ever. The very sentence that records the crime rehearses the promise it violates, and the historian, all agree, means the reader to feel the collision — the eternal name of the LORD and a carved idol, set under the same roof.
8:
Set against the mounting catalogue of sin, this verse recalls the ancient promise Manasseh was throwing away — "Neither will I make the feet of Israel move any more out of the land which I gave their fathers." The reformed John Gill hears in it God's pledge not to suffer his people to be carried captive out of their land, as they had been harried in the days of the judges, but a pledge, he stresses, resting upon the condition that follows. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the promise to its root in 2 Samuel 7:10, where God had said, "I will appoint my people a place, that they may dwell in a place of their own, and be stirred up no more," a word fulfilled in the building of the temple as the seat of the LORD's name, yet whose lasting fulfilment was made to depend upon Israel's faithful adherence to the commandments, exactly as the warning of 1 Kings 9:6 had set out.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the sharp inference the verse invites: had they been faithful to God's testimonies, they never would have gone into captivity, but should even at this day have been in possession of the promised land. The whole tragedy of the exile, on Clarke's reading, is folded into that unfulfilled "only if." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the cross-reference to the promise of 2 Samuel 7:10, taking the pledge that Israel's foot should "wander" no more to look back to the old migration into Egypt; the thought, he says, is that the permanent possession of the Promised Land depends wholly on the permanent adherence of the nation to Jehovah alone. He notes too the finer points of the text, that Chronicles and the versions read simply "to do all that I commanded," and that a stray "and" is best omitted with the parallel.

The witnesses gather, then, around the conditional heart of the verse. God's ancient generosity had been real and immense: a settled land, a chosen city, a temple for his name, and the promise of an untroubled dwelling. But every clause of it hung upon obedience, and that was the very thing Manasseh had cast off. The historian sets the gracious promise here, in the middle of the record of the king's abominations, so that the reader may see precisely what is being forfeited: not a bare threat of judgment, but the deliberate throwing away of a covenant that would have kept Israel for ever in the land, had they only kept the LORD. The mercy was sure; the condition was spurned; and the verses that follow will draw out the terrible arithmetic of that refusal.
9:
The gracious "only if" of the last verse meets its flat refusal here — "But they hearkened not" — and the witnesses weigh both the people's deafness and the king's active seduction. The reformed John Gill takes the failure at its plainest: they hearkened not to the voice of God in his law given by Moses, and would not be obedient to it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the deliberate breaking of the condition just laid down: Israel did not keep it, but Manasseh seduced them, so that they did more evil than the Canaanites whom Jehovah had destroyed before them, the very reversal that made the coming judgment just.

On that comparison, worse than the heathen, the witnesses press hard, and each finds a different edge to it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sees a king bending his whole power to corruption: he did all he could to pervert the national character and totally destroy the worship of the true God, "and he succeeded." John Wesley explains why Judah's guilt outran the Canaanites' in two ways: they were not content with the idols the Canaanites had worshipped but invented or borrowed many new ones from other nations; and their light was far clearer, their obligations to God infinitely higher, and their helps against idolatry much stronger, so that their sins, though the same in kind, were unspeakably worse for these dreadful aggravations. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lands on the same point from the theological side: the idolatry of Judah was worse than that of the Canaanites because the heathen worshipped only their own national gods, whereas Judah forsook its own God and stood ready to adopt almost any foreign cult it met, the very charge Jeremiah would bring in Jeremiah 2:11, that a nation had changed its glory for that which does not profit. Ellicott notes too that Chronicles softens the verb to "made them to err," and that the Septuagint adds "in the eyes of Jehovah."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the guilt of leader and led together: Manasseh not only sinned himself, having been well taught to know better, but debauched the people of God, whom he taught to sin and in effect forced to sin. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the sin of this reign is doubled — a people who would not hear, and a king who dragged them further than the very nations God had cast out — and that it is precisely this, the holy seed grown worse than the profane, which the prophets of the next verse are sent to arraign.
10:
Against the accumulated guilt God now sends his prophets, and the witnesses first ask who they were. The reformed John Gill, following the Jewish chronology, names three who prophesied in Manasseh's days: Joel, Nahum, and Habakkuk. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke widens the list to five — Hosea, Joel, Nahum, Habakkuk, and Isaiah — and takes the verses that follow to be the sum of what they spoke; he adds the old tradition that Isaiah was not only prophesying in those days but was put to death by Manasseh, sawn asunder with a wooden saw. The grammatical-historical witnesses are more cautious about names. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the prophets who foretold this terrible judgment are not named at all, though their utterances were entered in the annals according to 2 Chron. 33:18; Habakkuk, they think, was probably one, since he predicted the Chaldaean judgment as a thing incredible in Habakkuk 1:5.

Charles Ellicott sharpens the question over Isaiah. The general phrase "his servants the prophets" is used, he judges, precisely because the historian found no name in his source; it is possible that Isaiah was still living and protested against the apostasy, but more probably the protests were those of that great prophet's disciples, for the style of the following oracle is not Isaiah's. So the witnesses genuinely divide over the identity of the speakers: whether Isaiah himself stood among them, martyred for his witness, or whether the voices were an unnamed company of which Habakkuk was one and Isaiah's school another.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns from the men to their office, and reads the sending of the prophets as a graded mercy. Their first work was to teach the people the knowledge of God and remind them of their duty; failing that, to reprove them for their sins and set those sins before them, that they might repent and reform; and failing even that, to foretell the judgments of God, so that the terror of them might awaken to repentance those whom his love could not, or else that the execution of them in due season might prove the divine mission of the prophets who had foretold them. The prophets, Henry says, were thus deputed judges to those who would not receive them as teachers. The witnesses agree that this verse is the hinge of the chapter: mercy has spoken through the law and been refused, and now it speaks again through the prophets, arraigning a king and a people who have sinned against the clearest light, the last summons before the sentence of doom that the verses immediately following will pronounce.
11:
The indictment is now read out formally, and the witnesses attend to its terms — "because Manasseh hath done these abominations, and hath done wickedly above all that the Amorites did." The reformed John Gill refers the "abominations" simply to those already catalogued in the verses before. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes a small point of the Hebrew, that the "and" before "hath done wickedly" is not in the original, the sense running straight on, namely because he has done wickedly. More significantly, he explains the naming of the Amorites: it is a general designation of the native races of Canaan, much as Homer uses Achaeans or Danaans in turn for all the Greeks, a usage he traces through Amos 2:9, Ezekiel 16:3, and 1 Kings 21:26. Keil and Delitzsch read the Amorites the same way, named here instar omnium, as standing for all the supporters of Canaanite ungodliness, exactly as in 1 Kings 21:26 the phrase had branded the house of Ahab.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the aggravation the charge contains. Manasseh had done wickedly himself, though he knew better things, and had even justified the Amorites, the very nations God cast out, whose copy he wrote after, by outdoing them in impiety; and he had debauched the people of God, whom he taught to sin and forced to sin. The measure of the crime, Henry observes, is not merely that the king was wicked, but that he made a whole nation wicked, filling Jerusalem with idolatry and, as the later verse will add, with innocent blood.

The witnesses do not divide; their common concern is the logic of the "because." This verse is the ground of the sentence that the next will pronounce, and it rests the doom of Jerusalem not on any arbitrary severity but on a precisely stated cause: a king who had sinned above the heathen and dragged his people with him. The comparison to the Amorites is deliberate and damning: Judah had become worse than the peoples whose wickedness had once filled up the measure of their own destruction, so that the same rule of judgment which had emptied Canaan of its old inhabitants could now, in justice, be turned upon Jerusalem itself. The charge being read, the verdict must follow.
12:
The verdict falls — "Behold, I am bringing such evil upon Jerusalem and Judah, that whosoever heareth of it, both his ears shall tingle." The reformed John Gill pauses first on the speaker: it is "the LORD God of Israel" who pronounces the sentence, and though he is the covenant God, kind and gracious to Israel, he is also just and righteous and the sovereign Lord of all, so that the very title which had been Israel's comfort is here the guarantee of Israel's doom. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the vivid Hebrew of the tingling ears, a word formed to echo the very sensation it names, the ringing that follows a loud and shrill sound suddenly striking the ear; the prophets, he suggests, had spoken so vehemently that the sound seemed to go on ringing even after they had ceased, a faithful and solemn testimony that would not be shaken off.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same figure as the effect of dreadful news: the tidings shall strike the hearer like a sharp, piercing sound that leaves the ears ringing, the very metaphor used of coming judgments in 1 Samuel 3:11 and Jeremiah 19:3. He notes the textual variation, that where the received text has "his hearers" many manuscripts and the Hebrew margin read "her hearer," the city's own. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the whole into one impression: the judgment should be very terrible and amazing, so that the very report of it should make men's ears to tingle and their hearts tremble; it would make a great noise in the world and set every tongue speculating.

The witnesses do not contend, and the force of the verse lies in what they share. This is not a private disapproval but a public sentence, framed to be heard and to appall; the God who speaks it is the covenant God of Israel, which makes the coming ruin not the triumph of a rival power but the act of Judah's own Lord. The image of the tingling ears binds the verse to the great judgments of Israel's past, the fall of Eli's house and the breaking of Jerusalem, and warns that what is coming will belong in that dreadful company, a stroke so unheard-of that the mere telling of it will make the hearer flinch. The sentence is passed; the following verses will spell out its measure.
13:
The measure of the doom is now given in two images, a builder's line and plummet, and a housewife's dish, and the witnesses expound both. The reformed John Gill reads "the line of Samaria" with the Targum as the line of destruction: the same measure meted out to Samaria shall be measured to Jerusalem, the same lot, utter ruin. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the figure from its ordinary use: the measure and the plummet are applied both to what is being built up, as in Zechariah 1:16, and to what is being levelled to the ground, as in Amos 7:7, whence spring the figurative "measure of desolation" and "plummet of devastation" of Isaiah 34:11; the measure of Samaria is thus the measure once applied to Samaria's destruction, now stretched over Jerusalem.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the parallel out in full. As Samaria was taken, pillaged, ruined, and its people led into captivity, so Jerusalem shall have the same measure; and as the house of Ahab was totally destroyed, not a man of his race left to sit on Israel's throne, so shall the royal family of Judah be destroyed, not a man of their race left to sit on Judah's throne, the same weight as well as the same measure, because Jerusalem had copied all the abominations that ruined Samaria. On "I will wipe Jerusalem as a man wipeth a dish," Clarke offers a second image from the Vulgate, "I will blot out Jerusalem as tablets are wont to be blotted out," the wax tablet smoothed by the blunt end of the stile, so that God, who had once written Jerusalem down as the Holy City, now turns the stile and blots it out; Jerusalem is the dish turned upside down, the tablet erased, and so it has remained, Clarke says, to this present hour.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the sense, that Jerusalem shall be dealt with by the same rigorous rule of judgment already applied to Samaria, and reads the two household acts as one: the wiping of the dish represents the destruction of the people, the turning of it upside down the overthrow of the city, or, with Thenius, the two together simply express the single notion of making a complete end. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, comparing Isaiah 28:17, warns that those who resemble and imitate others in their sins must expect to fare as they fared; yet he finds one gleam in the grim image. The dish is not dropped, nor broken to pieces, nor melted down, but only wiped: the city is to be emptied of its inhabitants as a dish is emptied, and turned upside down, but with a view, at the last, to its purifying and not its final destruction. The witnesses do not divide; together they hear in the verse a sentence of total desolation upon Jerusalem, measured by the ruin of Samaria and the house of Ahab, and yet, in Henry's reading, not the shattering of the vessel, but its cleansing for a future use.
14:
The sentence deepens from ruin to abandonment — "I will forsake the remnant of mine inheritance, and deliver them into the hand of their enemies" — and the witnesses feel the weight of that word "remnant." The reformed John Gill explains it by the map of the divided kingdom: the whole land of Canaan was the LORD's inheritance, but ten tribes had already been carried away, so that only Judah with Benjamin was left; and now God threatens to forsake even that remnant. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads it the same way: one part, the ten tribes, was already forsaken and led into captivity, and the remnant, the tribe of Judah, was now about to be forsaken too. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms that the Northern Kingdom had already been depopulated, so that Judah stands as the last remnant of God's inheritance, now to be cast off, the Septuagint's stronger "I will thrust away," and made a prey and a spoil, in the language of Isaiah 42:22 and Jeremiah 30:16.

Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the climax of the threat, that with the destruction of Jerusalem the LORD forsakes the people of his possession and gives them up to their enemies for prey and spoil; but they set beside it, from the parallel in Chronicles, the astonishing sequel the book of Kings omits: that Jehovah caused Manasseh himself to be taken prisoner by the captains of the king of Assyria and led away to Babylon in chains; that there he humbled himself and made supplication; and that God brought him back to Jerusalem and his throne, after which he fortified the city, removed the idol from the temple, and abolished the idolatrous altars. The very king whose sin sealed the doom was himself, they note, a firstfruits of the mercy that would one day temper it, as 2 Chron. 33:11 and following record.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the moral of the forsaking with characteristic point: justly are those forsaken of God who first forsake him; nor does he ever leave any till they have first left him. But when God has forsaken a people, their defence is departed, and they become an easy prey to all their enemies. The witnesses do not divide over the sentence, though Keil and Delitzsch alone lift the reader's eye past it to the mercy stored up in the Chronicler's account. Together they read the verse as the darkest word yet, not merely defeat, but desertion, the withdrawal of the God whose presence had been Judah's only shield; and yet the same page of Scripture, in Manasseh's own captivity and restoration, quietly plants the hope that a God who forsakes for sin may yet return to the penitent.
15:
The ground of the sentence is now traced back across the whole national history — "because they have... provoked me to anger, since the day their fathers came forth out of Egypt, even unto this day." The reformed John Gill glosses the evil once more as idolatry, the settled sin of the people. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the tense: the Hebrew is not a single act but a continual habit, they have been provoking me, always; and he notes that the Septuagint preserves what may be the original reading, "since the day I brought forth their fathers out of Egypt," which throws the whole weight of the provocation against the background of the great deliverance it repaid with ingratitude.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the verse as the recital of old guilt come up for remembrance. Israel had provoked God from their very conception and birth as a people, from the day their fathers came out of Egypt; and the men of this generation, treading in their fathers' steps, are justly reckoned with for their fathers' sins as well as their own. The point is not that the present generation suffers for crimes not its own, but that it has ratified those crimes by repeating them, so that the long account, unbroken from the deliverance to Manasseh, is at last called in. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley guards the justice of it carefully: this forejudgment, though it was chiefly inflicted for the sins of Manasseh and his own generation, yet had a respect unto all their former sins, the accumulated provocation of centuries gathered into a single sentence.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse they expound is one of the most sweeping in the book. It refuses to let Judah imagine the coming ruin an accident of Manasseh's reign alone; it reaches back over the golden calf and the wilderness murmurings, the judges' apostasies and the calves of Jeroboam, to the very morning of the nation, and finds one long thread of provocation running through them all. Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment presses on toward the bloodshed of the next verse, treat this as the settled indictment behind it: a people who from the first had done evil in God's sight and provoked him to anger. The mercy had been ancient; so, the verse insists, had the sin; and the God who had borne with it "even unto this day" was now, in justice, bringing the long forbearance to its end.
16:
To idolatry the reign now adds slaughter — "Manasseh shed innocent blood very much, till he had filled Jerusalem from one end to another" — and the witnesses agree on whose blood it chiefly was. The reformed John Gill takes the innocent blood to be that of the prophets and the faithful whom Manasseh put to death for reproving his idolatry, and repeats the ancient report that Isaiah himself was among them, sawn asunder. The wesleyan-arminian witnesses concur: Adam Clarke paints the king as fierce and cruel, an unprincipled and merciless tyrant who, like the bloody deities he served, slew both his people and God's prophets; John Wesley reads the innocent blood as that of the prophets and righteous men who either reproved his sinful practices or refused to comply with his wicked commands, and notes that his "sin," the idolatry called sin by eminency, is set beside his cruelty as its root.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fill in the history and the horror. Keil and Delitzsch read "from one end to another" as filling Jerusalem edge to edge, and cite Josephus, who says Manasseh slew all the righteous among the Hebrews and did not spare even the prophets, killing some daily so that the city ran with blood. Charles Ellicott sets the killing in its social frame, quoting Robertson Smith: as the nation fell back into its old ways, a bloody struggle broke out between the conservative party and the followers of the prophets, and the king took the side of the reaction; the "innocent blood" was that of the prophets of Jehovah and their disciples.

Over one detail the witnesses openly differ. The tradition that Manasseh martyred Isaiah by having him sawn asunder, to which many find an allusion in Hebrews 11:37, "they were sawn asunder," is received by Gill and Wesley as sober fact and reported by Keil and Delitzsch as a widespread Jewish and Christian legend. Ellicott alone holds back: the Talmudic story that Isaiah was sawn asunder in the trunk of a cedar is, he judges, "perhaps not impossible, but hardly probable," though he grants with Ewald that Jeremiah 2:30, Psalms 141:7, and Isaiah 53 may allude to the persecution of the prophets in this reign. So the debate is narrow but real, whether the sawing of Isaiah is history or hallowed legend, while on the substance the witnesses stand together: Manasseh's idolatry issued, as it always will, in the persecution of those who would not bow to it, and it was this crowning guilt of innocent blood that filled up the measure and cried loudest for the vengeance already pronounced.
17:
The closing-formula of the reign — "the rest of the acts of Manasseh, and all that he did, and his sin that he sinned" — opens, for the witnesses, onto the one bright thing in this dark chapter, which the book of Kings itself withholds. The reformed John Gill notes that the "rest of his acts" were both good and bad, for Manasseh repented, and was humbled, and did many good things afterward, though they are not recorded in this book. The whole record of the king's recovery, that is, lies outside the historian's brief notice here, to be sought in the parallel account.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies that account in full from 2 Chron. 33:11 and following: the Assyrians took Manasseh, bound him with fetters, and carried him to Babylon; there he repented and sought God, and was, we are not told how, restored to his kingdom; he then fortified the city of David, destroyed idolatry, restored the worship of the true God, and died in peace. Clarke is careful, however, about the so-called Prayer of Manasseh, which stands in the Apocrypha: it contains some good sentiments, but whether it be the very prayer he made can by no means be proved, and even the Church of Rome has not received it among the canonical books. The fuller sources to which the verse points, the annals and the books of the seers, are, he adds, now lost.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the historical standing of that repentance, for it was once the fashion among critics to dismiss the Chronicler's story of Manasseh's captivity, penitence, and restoration as an edifying fiction. The best critics now, he reports, allow 2 Chron. 33:11-19 to be genuine history, a judgment that restores to this most wicked of Judah's kings the astonishing grace of a real conversion. The witnesses do not divide. The book of Kings, intent on the sin that sealed Jerusalem's doom, records only the crime; but Gill, Clarke, and Ellicott alike lift the reader's eye to the other volume, where the same Manasseh who filled Jerusalem with blood is found at last a humbled and pardoned man. It is the chapter's quiet reminder that no sinner, however deep in guilt, stands beyond the reach of the mercy that had spared even him.
18:
Manasseh's death and unusual burial close his reign — "he was buried in the garden of his own house, in the garden of Uzza" — and over the meaning of that garden-grave the witnesses genuinely divide. The reformed John Gill states the bare fact: the king slept with his fathers, that is, died, after a reign of fifty-five years and a life of sixty-seven. But why was he laid in a garden rather than in the royal sepulchres? The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley read the choice as the last testimony of a penitent. Being truly humbled for his sins, Henry says, Manasseh judged himself no more worthy to be called a son of David, and therefore not worthy to have even his dead body buried in the sepulchres of his fathers; true penitents take shame to themselves, and better it is to die repenting and be buried in a garden than to die impenitent and be buried in state. Wesley agrees that the garden-grave, chosen by the king's own command, was a lasting testimony of his sincere repentance and self-abhorrence for his former crimes.

The grammatical-historical witnesses see no penitence in it at all, only geography. Keil and Delitzsch argue that "his house" cannot be Solomon's royal palace, since the plot is also called the garden of Uzza after some former owner; it was rather a summer palace of Manasseh's, and the king was simply buried in its garden, the attempts of Thenius to fix its site on Ophel they dismiss as untenable, while Robinson would place it on Zion. Charles Ellicott likewise takes "his house" to be not the old palace but another that Manasseh had built for himself, and records Thenius's guess that the garden of Uzza lay in the Tyropoeon valley at the foot of Ophel, comparing the name Uzza with its occurrences in 2 Samuel 6:8, 1 Chronicles 8:7, and Nehemiah 7:51.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke stands between, reading the garden simply as the family burying-place, for Amon his son would be buried there too, as v.26 records. So the debate is real and unresolved: was the garden-grave a deliberate act of humble penitence, the king refusing the honour of the kings' tombs, or merely the ordinary burial-plot of a private palace? Henry and Wesley hear repentance in it; Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott hear only a location; Clarke a family custom. What none disputes is the turning of the page the verse also records — "and Amon his son reigned in his stead" — the passing of the crown to a son who, as the next verses show, would inherit the father's sins without the father's repentance.
19:
The reign of Amon opens, and the witnesses gather the facts of a short and inglorious kingship. The reformed John Gill fixes the dates: Amon was twenty-two years old when he began to reign, born in the forty-fifth year of his father's life and the thirty-third of his reign. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, struck that Manasseh's successor was a son not born until the father was forty-five, wonders aloud whether Manasseh, in his blind and brutish zeal for his idols, had sacrificed his elder sons in the fire, or whether, having been dedicated to those idols, they were refused by the people, a grim speculation, but one the child-sacrifice of v.6 makes not unnatural. Henry sums the reign in advance as short and inglorious: Amon forsook the God of his fathers, disobeyed the commands given them, disclaimed the covenant made with them, and walked in all the way his father had walked in.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notices a curious pattern in the two-year length of the reign. The rabbins' remark, he says, is not wholly without foundation, that the sons of idolatrous kings who succeeded their fathers seldom reigned more than two years — Nadab the son of Jeroboam in 1 Kings 15:25, Elah the son of Baasha in 1 Kings 16:8, Ahaziah the son of Ahab in 1 Kings 22:51, and now Amon the son of Manasseh — as though a swift judgment overtook the heirs of idolatry.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns to the names, and finds Egypt written in them. "Amon," read Amos in the Vatican Septuagint and by Josephus, is perhaps the name of the Egyptian sun-god Amen, the Greek Ammon, a fitting name for the son of an idolater; the whole age, Ellicott has noted, leaned toward Egypt against Assyria. The queen-mother's name, Meshullemeth, is the feminine of Meshullam, "friend," that is, of God, a form he illustrates from Isaiah 42:19; Ewald compares the Latin Pius and Pia used as proper names. Her town of Jotbah, Jerome says, lay in Judah, and a like name appears among the wilderness stations of Numbers 33:33 and Deuteronomy 10:7. The witnesses do not divide; together they introduce a king whose very name breathed the foreign idolatry he would practise, whose reign the rabbins reckoned cut short by judgment, and whose brief two years the following verses will fill with his father's sins and his own violent end.
20:
The verdict on Amon repeats the verdict on his father, almost word for word — "he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, as his father Manasseh did." The reformed John Gill glosses the evil, as so often in this chapter, in a single word: idolatry, the family sin now handed down another generation. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, reading the notice together with the verses that follow, sum Amon's reign as lived wholly in the spirit of his father: he worshipped all his father's idols, walked in all his father's ways, and forsook the LORD God of his fathers.

The comparison "as his father Manasseh did" carries, on the witnesses' reading, a peculiar sting, for by this point in the chapter Manasseh has become two men. There is the Manasseh of the early verses, who filled Jerusalem with idols and blood; and there is the Manasseh of v.17, the humbled penitent whom God had brought back from Babylon to tear down the very altars he had built. Amon, the witnesses note, copied not the father who repented but the father who sinned. The son had before him both the crime and the conversion, and he chose to imitate the crime, reviving the idolatry his father had, in the end, put away.

Neither witness labours the verse, for its brevity is part of its point: the historian has no new sins to catalogue, only the weary recurrence of the old. Where Manasseh's evil had been described at length, through high places and groves and the host of heaven, Amon's is dispatched in a clause, "as his father Manasseh did," as though the writer will not spend on the son the words he spent on the father. Yet the very economy is an indictment. Amon added nothing and repented of nothing; he simply took up the abominations his father had laid down, and in so short a reign as two years found time for no other record than this, that he did evil, exactly as his father once had done. The verses that follow will trace that evil into its detail and then to its sudden and bloody end.
21:
The evil of Amon is spelled out in its particulars — "he walked in all the way that his father walked in, and served the idols that his father served, and worshipped them." The reformed John Gill reads "all the ways" comprehensively: not idolatry only, but the whole wicked course of Manasseh's earlier reign, his idolatry, his witchcraft, and his murders, the full catalogue of the chapter's opening verses taken up again by the son. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the same clause, note simply that Amon walked in all his father's way and served and worshipped all his father's idols, a total, unqualified imitation.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws from the verse the lesson that most haunts it: Amon revived that very idolatry which Manasseh, in the latter end of his reign, had put down. And from this Wesley presses a sobering warning to every parent and teacher of others: those who set bad examples, though they may afterward repent themselves, cannot be sure that those whom their example has drawn into sin will likewise repent; it is often otherwise. Manasseh had sown the seed of idolatry across a whole generation, and though he lived to tear up his own planting, the harvest sprang up again in his son, beyond his power to recall.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse they expound turns the tragedy of the reign into a general truth. A father's late penitence cannot undo a father's early example; the idols Manasseh had served in the sight of his household were served again by the child who had watched him serve them, long after the old king had turned from them in tears. Gill sees in it the persistence of the whole evil course, idolatry, sorcery, and bloodshed, passing intact to the next reign; Wesley sees in it the melancholy law that sin taught is not so easily untaught as it was taught. Together they read Amon's reign as the bitter fruit of Manasseh's worst years, ripening after Manasseh himself had repented, a warning that the good a man does at the last may not overtake the harm he did at the first, and that example outlives its author.
22:
The heart of Amon's sin is named — "he forsook the LORD God of his fathers, and walked not in the way of the LORD" — and the witnesses read it as apostasy in its plainest form. The reformed John Gill hears in "the God of his fathers" the God of David and Solomon, the covenant God of the royal line, whom Amon deliberately abandoned. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch echo the charge without softening it: he forsook the LORD God of his fathers and walked not in his way. Charles Ellicott expands the single verb: Amon forsook Jehovah, abandoned his worship altogether, and gave himself up to the foreign superstitions his father had introduced.

Ellicott pauses here to take the measure of the whole Manasseh-Amon epoch, which the sacred historian, he observes, describes simply on the side of its relation to the religion of Israel. The astonishing corruption of worship that broke out in these reigns; the perverted hankering after foreign rites, which seems only to have been intensified by the restraints endured under Hezekiah; the bloody persecution of those who kept the ancient faith; and the prophetic menaces of coming retribution — such are the main points of the brief but impressive story. As usual, he adds, moral and religious licence went hand in hand. He calls the prophet Zephaniah to witness, who denounced all the ruling classes of "the rebellious and polluted city," princes and judges, prophets and priests alike involved in one condemnation in Zephaniah 1:4-5 and Zephaniah 3:1-4, and points too to the indictments of Micah 6:10 and following.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gathers up the theology of the whole reign. Amon's crime is not described as a set of separate offences but as one act of desertion: he forsook the God of his fathers. Gill roots that desertion in a contempt of the covenant line, Ellicott in a hankering after every foreign cult, Keil and Delitzsch in the bare refusal to walk in the LORD's way; but all three see a king who turned his back deliberately upon the God who had made and kept his house. It is the same forsaking that God had threatened, in reverse, at v.14 — "I will forsake the remnant of mine inheritance" — and the witnesses leave the reader to feel the dreadful symmetry: a people and a king who forsake the LORD are, in the end, forsaken by him.
23:
Amon's reign ends abruptly in blood — "the servants of Amon conspired against him, and slew the king in his own house" — and the witnesses probe both the motive of the murder and its meaning. On the motive they can only conjecture, and they conjecture differently. The reformed John Gill supposes the assassins were some of his domestic servants, perhaps his courtiers, who struck not on account of his idolatry but for some ill usage they had suffered at his hands, a private grievance, not a religious one. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads it likewise as personal: his own servants, who should have guarded him, murdered him, probably upon some personal disgust, and his own house, which should have been his castle of defence, became the place of his execution. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke confesses the cause simply unknown — "what their reason was for slaying their king we cannot tell" — while noting that the deed was plainly not a popular one, since the people would shortly rise and avenge it. Charles Ellicott agrees that the "servants" were, by the usual sense of the word, his courtiers or palace officials, that nothing further is known of the circumstances, and that for any conjecture one must look to the note at 2 Chron. 33:25.

On the meaning of the murder, however, Henry hears a solemn justice beneath the crime. Amon had profaned God's house with his idols, and now God suffered his own house to be polluted with his blood; and however unrighteous the men were who did it, God was righteous who suffered it to be done. The place of the king's sin became the place of the king's death, a symmetry Henry will not let the reader miss.

The witnesses do not finally divide, for their disagreement is only over a motive none can recover; on the fact and its weight they stand together. Amon had reigned but two years, long enough to revive his father's idols and to earn his father's guilt, but not long enough, as Clarke pointedly observes, to copy his father's repentance. He was struck down unrepentant, in the very palace he had filled with false worship, by the hands of his own household. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative straight on: the conspiracy would not stand, for the people of the land were about to answer blood with blood, and out of the wreck of this violent reign would come, unlooked for, the accession of one of Judah's best kings.
24:
The murder is answered by the nation, and the throne passes to a child who would prove Judah's hope — "the people of the land slew all them that had conspired against king Amon; and the people of the land made Josiah his son king in his stead." The reformed John Gill reads the people's rising as an act of vengeance: on Amon's death they gathered in a body to avenge their king, whether because his idolatry had made him beloved of a depraved nation, or simply because, bad as he was, he was their king, whose life his servants had no right to take. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sees in it two things done at once. First, the people did justice on the traitors and put them to death; for though Amon was a bad king, he was their king, and it was part of their allegiance to avenge his death, thereby clearing themselves of any share in the crime and deterring others from the like. Second, they did a kindness to themselves in making Josiah his son king, whom the conspirators had perhaps meant to set aside, but whom the people stood by and settled on the throne.

The grammatical-historical witnesses attend to the phrase "the people of the land." Keil and Delitzsch take it to mean the general population of Judah, comparing its use at 2 Chronicles 26:1, and note that they set the boy Josiah, only eight years old, upon his father's throne. Charles Ellicott records and rejects a narrower reading: Thenius thought "the people of the land" were the militia, as he took them to be at 2 Kings 11:14, but in neither place, Ellicott judges, does that opinion appear likely; the phrase means the body of the nation, not an armed class.

The witnesses do not divide, and they let the verse open toward light. Henry hears in it a happy change from one of the worst to one of the best of all the kings of Judah, and God's own word of patience: "Once more they shall be tried with a reformation." Out of the assassination of an idolater, the people's rough justice, and the crowning of a mere child, God was quietly preparing the great reforming reign of Josiah. The chapter that has been unrelieved darkness, Manasseh's abominations, the sentence of doom, Amon's brief and bloody evil, turns here, in its closing verses, toward the one king who would tear down what these two had built; and the witnesses, following the historian, let the reader feel the dawn breaking at the very end of the night.
25:
The reign of Amon is closed with the book's standing formula — "Now the rest of the acts of Amon which he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?" — and the reformed John Gill passes over it with the briefest of notes, a bare cross-reference back to the same formula already explained. There is, in truth, little for the witnesses to say, and the poverty of the notice is itself eloquent. Where the identical formula had stood over Manasseh at v.17, Gill had found in the father's "rest of his acts" both good and bad, a record of repentance, of humbling, of many good things done after his return from Babylon. Here, over the son, the same words enclose nothing of the kind.

For Amon's two years held no such reversal. As the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke had observed a few verses before, Amon was as bad as his father in the beginning of his reign, but he "did not copy his father's repentance"; and so the annals to which this verse points can record, for all his brief kingship, only the evil already told, the idols served, the LORD forsaken, the way of David abandoned. The closing-formula, which for a good king gathers up wars and works and wisdom, here gathers up almost nothing: a reign too short and too wicked to leave much history behind it.

The contrast the verse quietly draws is the chapter's own. Manasseh sinned more deeply than Amon ever did, filling Jerusalem with idols and with blood; yet Manasseh repented, and his formula could hold a measure of good. Amon sinned less, but died unrelenting, and his formula holds none. The witnesses, following the historian, let the bare notice make its point without comment: it is not the depth of a man's sin alone that the record finally weighs, but whether, like the father, he turned from it at the last, or, like the son, was cut off in it. The formula that seems merely to defer to the royal chronicles becomes, set beside v.17, a silent verdict on a wasted and impenitent reign; and the narrative hastens on to the burial that will lay Amon beside the father whose sins, but not whose penitence, he had made his own.
26:
The chapter ends at a graveside that is also a threshold — "he was buried in his sepulchre in the garden of Uzza: and Josiah his son reigned in his stead." The reformed John Gill notes only that Amon was laid in the sepulchre in the garden of Uzza, where his father Manasseh had been buried before him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds that it was a tomb Amon had caused to be prepared near his father's, recalling his earlier note at v.16 — the idolatrous father and the idolatrous son lying together in the private garden that had become their family sepulchre.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke seizes the mention of the garden to settle a question that has run through the chapter, what the "grove" of the earlier verses really was. Every occurrence of the word Asherah, he argues at length, denotes an idol and not a plantation of trees. He marshals the authorities: Jarchi, who on Genesis 12:3 takes Asherah for a tree worshipped by the Gentiles, as the oak was worshipped by the Druids; Castel, who defines it as a wooden image dedicated to Astarte or Venus; and Theodoret, who reads the Septuagint's alsos as Astarte and Venus. Above all, Clarke appeals to the reforms of Josiah, for when it is said in 2 Kings 23:6 that the king "brought out the grove from the house of the LORD," no literal grove could have been growing in the temple; the plain sense is that he brought out the idol-goddess Asherah and burnt it. So too the women who "wove hangings for the grove" in 2 Kings 23:7 were weaving shrines for Asherah, as the silversmiths made shrines for Diana in Acts 19:24; and the Syriac and Arabic versions render the word plainly "idol." Asherah, Clarke concludes, was none other than Venus, whose worship the mention of whoremongers and prostitutes lays bare.

The witnesses do not divide, and the chapter's last word is one of hope breaking over a grave. Amon is buried beside his father, the second idolater laid in the garden of Uzza; but the same verse that closes the impenitent reign opens another — "and Josiah his son reigned in his stead." The boy set on the throne by the people of the land will be the great reformer who tears down the high places, burns the Asherah Clarke has just been describing, and defiles the very altars Manasseh built. The historian, having led the reader through the deepest darkness in Judah's story, ends on the name of the king who would, for a season, roll it back, so that the chapter of doom closes, quietly, on the promise of a reformation to come.
